title: Reading Financial News Like an Accountant
subhead: An accountant who can't connect business news to accounting treatment is operating with one hand tied behind their back — and it impresses the hell out of interviewers. Five headlines, the journal entries behind each, and the language CFOs use on earnings calls.
hook: "So, did you see what the Fed did Tuesday?" In an interview that's a soft screening question — do you follow the news, and can you connect it to the books? Most candidates freeze. After this module, you can name the journal entry.

chunks.title: The Wall Street Journal for Accountants — Translating Headlines into Journal Entries
chunks.bodyHtml:
Headline 1: "Fed Raises Interest Rates by 0.25%"

Accounting impact: variable-rate debt (like a line of credit) now carries more interest expense next month — accrue more. The fair value of fixed-rate bonds on your balance sheet decreases (relevant if you hold an investment portfolio).

Skarion impact: its $500K term loan at 6.5% — if variable, a 0.25% hike = $1,250 more interest/year ≈ $104/month. Post more Dr Interest Expense / Cr Accrued Interest.

Headline 2: "Tech Firm X Files for Chapter 11 Bankruptcy"

Accounting impact: if they owe you money (they're in your AR), write that receivable off now. Don't wait.

Skarion impact: if TeleCom Builders files Chapter 11, the $112,700 balance → Dr Allowance for Doubtful Accounts / Cr Accounts Receivable immediately.

Headline 3: "Company Y Announces Acquisition of Company Z for $800M"

Accounting impact: the acquirer records the acquired company's assets at fair value; any excess of purchase price over fair value of net assets becomes Goodwill. Goodwill is not amortized under GAAP but tested annually for impairment.

Headline 4: "Inflation Running at 4.2% — Input Costs Rising"

Accounting impact: COGS rises faster than prices = margin compression. Inventory-carrying businesses may need to weigh LIFO vs FIFO more carefully; services firms like Skarion feel it through wage pressure — review the offshore MSA rates and the payroll budget.

Headline 5: "Cryptocurrency Exchange Collapses"

Accounting impact: any crypto held as an asset must be marked to fair value immediately. Under ASC 350-60 (the 2024 FASB rule), crypto now requires fair-value accounting — losses and gains flow to the income statement.

cards.back: Variable-rate debt accrues more interest — Dr Interest Expense / Cr Accrued Interest (Skarion's $500K loan ≈ +$104/month if variable). Fixed-rate bond fair values on your balance sheet also decline.

Write the AR off now — Dr Allowance for Doubtful Accounts / Cr Accounts Receivable. Don't wait for final settlement; the receivable is impaired the day they file. Skarion + TeleCom Builders = $112,700.
Acquirer records acquired net assets at fair value; the premium over fair value becomes Goodwill (an intangible asset). Goodwill is NOT amortized under GAAP — it's tested annually for impairment.

Margin compression: COGS rises faster than prices. Inventory businesses revisit LIFO/FIFO; services firms like Skarion face wage pressure and renegotiated offshore rates. Watch gross margin month-over-month.
Crypto held as an asset is marked to fair value immediately — Dr Impairment Loss / Cr Crypto Asset. Under ASC 350-60 (2024 FASB), losses and gains now flow through the income statement.

chunks.title: Quarterly Earnings Calls — What the CFO Is Actually Saying
chunks.bodyHtml:
Investor language isn't jargon for its own sake — each phrase points at a number the accounting team maintains. Learning it makes you a better communicator at every level.

  1. "Revenue grew 18% YoY" — year-over-year. You need to know how to calculate it and what drives it: volume vs. price vs. mix. (A services firm's growth should be split between more jobs vs higher rates vs a shift toward higher-margin deliverables.)
  2. "EBITDA margin expanded 200 basis points" — a basis point is 0.01%, so 200 bps = 2%. EBITDA margin went from, say, 22% to 24%. EBITDA strips out interest, tax, depreciation, and amortization — a cleaner view of operating performance.
  3. "We saw a $4M headwind from FX" — foreign-exchange impact. Revenue in multiple currencies is affected by currency moves; a strengthening dollar reduces reported USD revenue from foreign operations. You hedge or explain it.
  4. "Organic growth of 12% excluding the Westfield acquisition" — stripping out growth from acquisitions shows what the underlying business did on its own. Accountants maintain the segment-level data that makes organic vs. inorganic growth separable.
  5. "We're reaffirming guidance" — the company's own public forecast for next quarter/year. The accountant maintains the financial model that feeds this guidance, which is exactly what you'll build in Module 13.

chunks.title: Read a Mock CFO Earnings Commentary — 3 Questions
chunks.intro: Read this Skarion Q1 commentary, then answer. (Mock commentary: "Skarion delivered Q1 revenue of $1.44M, up 18% from $1.22M in the prior-year quarter, driven by a 14% increase in billed miles and a 3.5% improvement in average price per mile. EBITDA margin expanded 200 basis points to 52%, helped primarily by the Tennessee Corridor segment's contribution. Excluding the broadline contract win, organic growth was 9%.")

questions.q: What was Skarion's YoY revenue growth, and approximately how much of it came from volume (miles) vs price?
options: ~18% total — ~14% from billed-miles volume, ~3.5% from price (rest = mix)
questions.explanation: $1.44M vs $1.22M = ~18% YoY. The commentary attributes ~14% to miles (volume) and ~3.5% to price — the residual small slice is mix/refinement. Splitting growth by driver is exactly what an accountant does behind the headline.

questions.q: If Skarion's prior-year EBITDA margin was 50%, what does 'EBITDA margin expanded 200 basis points to 52%' actually mean?
options: EBITDA margin rose from 50% to 52% (200 bps = 2 percentage points)
questions.explanation: 200 basis points = 2.0 percentage points. So margin moved from the prior year's 50% to 52%. Basis points exist to remove the ambiguity between '% of revenue' and 'percent change.'

questions.q: The commentary credits 'the Tennessee Corridor segment' as the biggest contributor to margin improvement. As the accountant, what data do you maintain that proves this claim?
options: A segment-level (job-cost) P&L showing each project's revenue, COGS, and gross margin — exactly the job-costing work from Module 11
questions.explanation: The claim is segment-attributable, so the proof is the job-cost P&L: Tennessee Corridor vs Texas Long-Haul vs Virginia Metro, each with its own revenue, COGS, and margin. Same job-cost work from Mod 11 — which segment drove margin expansion is a narrative built on that data.
